questions.question: What were the major limitations of AWT (Abstract Window Toolkit) that led to the development of Swing?
questions.hint: Focus on heavyweight vs lightweight and platform dependency issues
keyPoints: AWT used heavyweight components that relied on native peer components
AWT components were platform-dependent causing inconsistent appearance across different operating systems
Limited widget set - only basic components like Button, TextField, Checkbox were available
Poor graphical capabilities and limited look-and-feel customization
Heavyweight components blocked underlying pixels causing painting issues

questions.question: Explain the term 'heavyweight' component in the context of Java GUI programming.
questions.hint: Think about the relationship between Java objects and native OS components
keyPoints: Heavyweight components are associated with native peer objects from the underlying operating system
They depend on the underlying platform's native GUI toolkit for rendering
Each heavyweight component has its own opaque window separate from the Java container
They cannot be transparent or have irregular shapes
Multiple heavyweight components cannot overlap properly due to Z-order issues

questions.question: How did Swing address the platform-dependency issue of AWT?
questions.hint: Focus on lightweight components and pluggable look-and-feel
keyPoints: Swing introduced lightweight components that do not use native peer objects
Swing components are rendered entirely in Java using Graphics2D API
Swings uses pluggable look-and-feel (PLAF) to mimic native OS appearance
Components can be rendered differently based on the chosen look-and-feel
Same code produces consistent output across all platforms

questions.question: What is the relationship between Swing and AWT? Why is AWT still needed with Swing?
questions.hint: Consider Swing as an extension built upon AWT foundation
keyPoints: Swing is built on top of AWT infrastructure
Swing uses AWT for event handling, layout managers, and graphics operations
The java.awt package still provides fundamental classes like Graphics, Color, Font
AWT's event delegation model is used by Swing for handling user interactions
Layout managers from AWT are reused for positioning Swing components

questions.question: Differentiate between heavyweight and lightweight components in Java.
questions.hint: Compare native dependency and rendering approach
keyPoints: Heavyweight: uses native peer objects from OS, platform-dependent, creates separate window
Lightweight: purely Java-based, no native peers, shares container's window
Heavyweight components always appear on top of lightweight components
Lightweight components can be transparent and have irregular shapes
Swing components are primarily lightweight with some heavyweight containers

questions.question: When and why was Swing introduced in Java?
questions.hint: Remember JDK 1.2 and Java 2 Platform milestones
keyPoints: Swing was introduced as part of Java Foundation Classes (JFC) in 1997
It was developed to overcome AWT's significant limitations
Swing was first included in JDK 1.2 as part of the Java 2 Platform
The main goal was to provide rich, platform-independent GUI components
It aimed to offer better visual appeal and functionality than AWT

questions.question: Discuss the evolution of Java GUI frameworks from AWT to Swing, highlighting the problems that led to this evolution.
questions.hint: Cover timeline, specific problems with AWT, and Swing innovations
keyPoints: AWT (1995): First Java GUI toolkit using native peers, limited components
Problems with AWT: heavyweight components, platform-dependent look
AWT had only about 12-15 basic components severely limiting GUI design
Inconsistent appearance across platforms frustrated developers
Introduction of JFC/Swing in 1997 with lightweight, sophisticated components
Swing provided 100+ components including tables, trees, tabbed panes
Swing introduced Model-View-Controller architecture for better separation
Pluggable look-and-feel allowed runtime switching of visual styles

questions.question: Compare AWT and Swing under the following headings: (i) Component type (ii) Look and Feel (iii) Customization (iv) Performance
questions.hint: Structure answer clearly under each heading with contrasting points
keyPoints: (i) Component type: AWT uses heavyweight (native peers), Swing uses lightweight (pure Java)
(ii) Look and Feel: AWT uses native OS appearance, Swing can mimic any look-and-feel
(ii) Swing has pluggable look-and-feel allowing Metal, Windows, Motif, GTK+ styles
(iii) Customization: AWT offers limited customization, Swing offers extensive customization
(iii) Swing components support borders, icons, tooltips, undo/redo
(iv) Performance: AWT is faster due to native code, Swing is slower initially but improved with hardware acceleration
(iv) Swing has double buffering reducing visual flicker

questions.question: Explain the design philosophy behind Swing. How does it achieve platform independence while still providing native look-and-feel?
questions.hint: Focus on lightweight architecture and PLAF mechanism
keyPoints: Swing was designed with three key principles: lightweight, modular, configurable
Lightweight components are completely written in Java without native peers
Platform independence achieved through pure Java rendering using Graphics2D
Pluggable Look-and-Feel (PLAF) architecture allows runtime switching
UI delegates handle actual rendering based on selected look-and-feel
Default look-and-feel can mimic native OS (Windows, GTK+, Motif)
Components are composed of API (model), delegate (UI), and peer (optional)
The UIManager class manages look-and-feel switching at runtime

questions.question: What is the Model-View-Controller (MVC) architecture in Swing? How does Swing's implementation differ from classical MVC?
questions.hint: Explain the Model-Delegate pattern specific to Swing
keyPoints: MVC separates data (Model), presentation (View), and control logic (Controller)
Classical MVC: Model notifies View of changes, View updates based on Model
Swing implements a modified MVC called Model-Delegate or MVC-lite
In Swing, the View and Controller are combined into a single UI object
Swing components have separate Model interface and UI delegate
Example: JTable has TableModel for data and TableUI for rendering
This simplification reduces complexity while maintaining separation
Models are pluggable allowing different data representations

questions.question: Design a simple Java program demonstrating the transition from AWT to Swing by creating equivalent buttons in both toolkits.
questions.hint: Write clean code showing direct comparison between AWT and Swing
keyPoints: AWT: Frame frame = new Frame('AWT Example'); Button btn = new Button('Click Me');
AWT: frame.add(btn); frame.setSize(300, 200); frame.setVisible(true);
Swing: JFrame frame = new JFrame('Swing Example'); JButton btn = new JButton('Click Me');
Swing: frame.add(btn); frame.setSize(300, 200); frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
Key differences: JFrame vs Frame, JButton vs Button, setDefaultCloseOperation
Swing uses invokeLater for thread safety in GUI creation
Swing button supports icons, tooltips, keyboard mnemonics
Show that Swing code is simpler and more feature-rich

questions.question: Consider an AWT application with 5 heavyweight buttons on a Frame. If each button creates its own native window, explain how many windows are created in total and why this causes Z-order issues with lightweight components.
questions.hint: Count the Frame as one heavyweight window plus each button
keyPoints: 5 buttons + 1 Frame = 6 heavyweight native windows created
Each AWT button has an associated native peer from the OS
These native windows appear on top of any lightweight components
Lightweight components cannot overlap heavyweight peers properly
The Z-order problem: heavyweight always appears above lightweight
This forces developers to either use all heavyweight or all lightweight
Solution: Use all-Swing (lightweight) applications to avoid this issue

questions.question: A developer wants to create a GUI with a transparent button over an image. Analyze whether this is possible using (a) AWT only (b) Swing components. Justify your answer.
questions.hint: Focus on transparency capabilities of heavyweight vs lightweight
keyPoints: (a) AWT: NOT possible with standard Button class
AWT buttons are heavyweight and create opaque native windows
They block visibility of any component or image behind them
Heavyweight components cannot be made transparent
(b) Swing: POSSIBLE with JButton
JButton is lightweight and can have transparent backgrounds
setContentAreaFilled(false) makes background transparent
setOpaque(false) allows background colors to show through
Lightweight components can have irregular shapes using setShape()

questions.question: Write Java code snippets to switch between Metal, Windows, and Motif look-and-feel in a Swing application. Also explain the default look-and-feel behavior.
questions.hint: Remember to handle exceptions and apply changes to existing components
keyPoints: Import: import javax.swing.UIManager;
Metal: UIManager.setLookAndFeel('javax.swing.plaf.metal.MetalLookAndFeel')
Windows: UIManager.setLookAndFeel('com.sun.java.swing.plaf.windows.WindowsLookAndFeel')
Motif: UIManager.setLookAndFeel('com.sun.java.swing.plaf.motif.MotifLookAndFeel')
Use try-catch for ClassNotFoundException and InstantiationException
Call SwingUtilities.updateComponentTreeUI(frame) to apply changes
Default: System look-and-feel if available, otherwise Metal
Cross-platform default is Metal (Java's own look-and-feel)

questions.question: List at least 5 GUI components that were available in AWT and their improved Swing equivalents.
questions.hint: Remember J prefix for Swing components and their enhanced features
keyPoints: Button → JButton (supports icons, tooltips, keyboard mnemonics)
Label → JLabel (supports HTML text, icons, alignment)
TextField → JTextField (more features, document filtering)
Checkbox → JCheckBox (improved visual, tri-state option)
Choice → JComboBox (dropdown with editable option)
List → JList (multiple selection, cell rendering)
TextArea → JTextArea (document model, undo/redo support)
